matter.title: Roe v. Fairbanks et al. (Timber Trespass)
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe sues neighbor Maria Q. Fairbanks and logging contractor Coastal Outfitters LLC after the defendants cut and hauled away trees from a mature white pine and hemlock woodlot on Roe's land, seeking the value of the timber plus enhanced damages under Maine's timber-trespass statute and a determination of the true boundary.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe sues neighbor Maria Q. Fairbanks and logging contractor Coastal Outfitters LLC after the defendants cut and hauled away trees from a mature white pine and hemlock woodlot on Roe's land, seeking the value of the timber plus enhanced damages under Maine's timber-trespass statute and a determination of the true boundary.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe owns a mature white pine and hemlock woodlot in Sagadahoc County, Maine. On or about 2024-05-31, a logging crew working for Coastal Outfitters LLC entered the property and cut roughly 589 trees, hauling the logs off to a sawmill for sale. The clearing was ordered by the adjoining neighbor, Maria Q. Fairbanks, who had hired Coastal Outfitters LLC to harvest timber along the parties' shared boundary.

Grace never authorized any cutting. A consulting forester later appraised the stumpage value of the felled trees at roughly $165269 and estimated the cost to restore the woodlot at about $159501. The character of the harvest matters: ordinary woodlot, no shoreland issue. Coastal Outfitters LLC contends the trees stood on Fairbanks's side of the true line, which puts the location of the boundary squarely in dispute.

Grace brings suit in Sagadahoc County Superior Court against both the neighbor who ordered the cutting and the contractor who carried it out. Grace seeks the value of the trees together with the enhanced double and treble damages Maine law allows for timber trespass, the cost of restoration, and a judgment quieting title and fixing the true boundary between the parcels.
undisputed_facts: Trees on or near the parties' shared boundary were cut on or about 2024-05-31 and the logs were hauled to a sawmill.
Maria Q. Fairbanks hired Coastal Outfitters LLC to perform the harvest.
Grace C. Roe did not sign any written authorization for cutting on Roe's land.
disputed_facts: Whether the cut trees stood on Roe's land or on Fairbanks's side of the true boundary.
Whether the cutting was intentional or knowing for purposes of treble damages under 14 M.R.S. § 7552.
The stumpage value of the trees and whether restoration cost or diminution in value is the proper measure of damages.

timeline.event: The logging crew cut and removed trees along the parties' shared boundary.
Complaint for timber trespass and to quiet title prepared for filing in Bath Superior Court.

third_parties.name: Northstar Partners Corp.
third_parties.relevance: Took delivery of the cut logs and holds the scale and delivery tickets.

third_parties.name: Maine Forest Service
third_parties.relevance: May review the unlicensed harvest and any forest-practices violations.

third_parties.name: Wesley B. Fairbanks
third_parties.relevance: Appraised the stumpage and the damage to the woodlot.

exchanges.question: Tell me what happened to your land.
exchanges.answer: I came up to the woodlot and found 589 of my trees cut and gone. Maria next door had hired a logging outfit, and they cut right across my line and hauled my timber off to a mill.

exchanges.question: Did you ever give anyone permission to cut on your property?
exchanges.answer: Never. Nobody asked me, and I never signed anything. The first I knew of it was the stumps and the skidder ruts.

exchanges.question: Is the property line between you and the neighbor in question?
exchanges.answer: They are claiming the trees were on Maria's side, but I do not believe it. I want a surveyor to establish the true line so this is settled for good.

exchanges.question: Do you have a sense of what the trees were worth?
exchanges.answer: A forester walked the cut and put the stumpage at around $165269, and restoring the lot could run about $159501. I understand the timber-trespass statute can multiply that.

exchanges.question: Who do you think should be held accountable?
exchanges.answer: Both of them. Maria ordered it, and the logging contractor is the one who actually cut my trees without checking the line. I want them both on the hook.

exchanges.question: What outcome are you hoping for?
exchanges.answer: Full value of my trees with the enhanced damages, the cost to restore the woodlot, and a clear ruling on where the boundary really is.

goals: Recover the full value of the cut trees plus the enhanced double or treble damages allowed under 14 M.R.S. § 7552.
Recover the cost to restore and replant the woodlot.
Establish the true boundary and hold both the neighbor and the logging contractor accountable.
priorities: Maximizing the timber-trespass recovery, including the enhanced-damages multiplier.
A definitive determination of the boundary line so it cannot be relitigated.
Holding both the neighbor who ordered the cut and the contractor who did it responsible.
desired_outcomes: A judgment for the value of the timber, doubled or trebled under the statute, plus restoration cost.
A quiet-title judgment fixing the true boundary in Roe's favor.
non_starters: A resolution that leaves the boundary unsettled or excuses the contractor who cut the trees.

constraints.budget: Moderate; prefers a strong settlement but will try the enhanced-damages claim if needed.
constraints.timeline: Wants the boundary fixed and the timber claim resolved before the next harvest season.

issues.issue: Quiet title and determination of the true boundary line where the cutter claims the trees were on his land
sub_questions: Where does the true boundary between the parcels lie?
Do the deeds, monuments, and survey place the cut trees on the plaintiff's land?
issues.client_position: The deeds and a proper survey place the cut trees on Roe's side of the true line.

issues.issue: Whether restoration cost or diminution in value is the proper measure of damages
sub_questions: Is the cost of restoration the appropriate measure for the damage to the woodlot?
Does restoration cost exceed the diminution in the land's value?

issues.issue: Shoreland-zoning buffer violation where the cut crossed a protected zone
sub_questions: Did the cutting violate the shoreland buffer or timber-harvesting standards?
What municipal or state enforcement consequences follow?

issues.issue: Liability and enhanced damages for timber trespass under Maine's cutting-of-trees statute
sub_questions: May the owner recover the value of the trees plus double damages for the unauthorized cutting?
Was the cutting intentional or knowing so that up to treble damages apply?
issues.client_position: The cutting was unauthorized, so Grace is entitled to the value of the trees plus enhanced double, and up to treble, damages.

issues.issue: Conversion of the cut timber once it was severed and hauled away
sub_questions: Did the defendants exercise dominion over the plaintiff's severed timber?
Is the sawmill that took delivery liable for converting the logs?
issues.client_position: Once cut, the logs were Roe's personal property, and removing and selling them was conversion.

expert_opinions.topic: Location of the true boundary line
expert_opinions.opinion: The deeds and monuments place the cut trees on the plaintiff's side of the true boundary.
expert_opinions.basis: Boundary survey, record deeds, and located monuments.

expert_opinions.topic: Stumpage value and cost of restoration
expert_opinions.opinion: The felled trees carried substantial stumpage value and the woodlot can be restored only at significant cost.
expert_opinions.basis: Stump count and measurement, species and grade analysis, and stumpage pricing.

evidence.title: Forester's stumpage and damage appraisal
evidence.description: The consulting forester's appraisal of stumpage value and restoration cost.

evidence.title: Photographs of the stumps and cleared area
evidence.description: Photographs of the cut stumps, skid trails, and the cleared portion of the woodlot.

evidence.title: Boundary survey
evidence.description: The surveyor's plan locating the true boundary between the parcels.

evidence.title: Deed and recorded plan
evidence.description: The recorded deed and plan establishing the plaintiff's title and the boundary.

amounts.label: Stumpage value of the cut trees
amounts.basis: Forester's appraisal of the felled timber.

amounts.label: Double / treble enhanced damages
amounts.basis: Statutory multiplier on the value of the trees under 14 M.R.S. § 7552.

amounts.label: Boundary survey cost
amounts.basis: Cost of the boundary survey to locate the true line.

causes_of_action.claim: Timber trespass with enhanced damages under 14 M.R.S. § 7552
causes_of_action.against: Maria Q. Fairbanks and Coastal Outfitters LLC
elements: The defendants cut or caused the cutting without permission
The owner may recover the value of the trees plus double damages
Up to treble damages apply where the cutting was intentional or knowing
causes_of_action.relief_sought: The value of the trees plus double damages, and up to treble damages for intentional or knowing cutting

elements: The plaintiff owned or possessed the land
The defendant intentionally entered the land
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Damages for the unauthorized entry and disturbance of the land

causes_of_action.against: Coastal Outfitters LLC and the sawmill
elements: The plaintiff owned the severed timber
The defendants exercised dominion over it
The exercise of dominion was wrongful
Resulting damages equal to the value of the timber
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Damages equal to the value of the converted logs

causes_of_action.claim: Quiet title and determination of the boundary
elements: The plaintiff holds record title to the parcel
An actual controversy exists over the boundary line
The deeds and monuments fix the true line
A judgment is needed to settle title and the boundary
causes_of_action.relief_sought: A judgment quieting title and declaring the true boundary between the parcels

affirmative_defenses.defense: The trees were on the defendant's land
affirmative_defenses.basis: The cut trees stood on Fairbanks's side of the true boundary, so no trespass occurred. 14 M.R.S. § 6651.
Any damages should be reduced by the plaintiff's own failure to mark or maintain the boundary. 14 M.R.S. § 156.

affirmative_defenses.defense: Good-faith reliance on a flagged line
affirmative_defenses.basis: The contractor reasonably and in good faith cut to a boundary line that had been flagged, negating any intentional or knowing cutting.

counterclaims.claim: Declaratory judgment that the boundary favors the neighbor
counterclaims.relief: A declaration that the true boundary lies so that the cut trees stood on the defendant's land.

discovery.description: Plaintiff's forester and surveyor disclosures and reports.
Deposition of the neighbor who ordered the clearing.
Requests for the logging contract and the sawmill scale tickets.
Deposition of the logging contractor who cut the trees.

motions.title: Motion in limine on valuation
motions.relief: Exclusion of the plaintiff's restoration-cost valuation.

motions.title: Motion for partial summary judgment on liability
motions.relief: Judgment that the unauthorized cutting establishes timber-trespass liability.

motions.title: Motion to determine the boundary
motions.relief: A determination of the true boundary between the parcels.

motions.title: Motion for the enhanced-damages multiplier
motions.relief: An order applying the double or treble damages multiplier under 14 M.R.S. § 7552.

docket.entry: Complaint for timber trespass and to quiet title filed.
Answers of the neighbor and the logging contractor filed.
Counterclaim for declaratory judgment on the boundary filed.
Scheduling order entered; discovery and survey deadlines set.
Hearing on partial summary judgment and the boundary held.
Expert reports on stumpage value and the boundary served.

facts.woodlot_type: a mature white pine and hemlock woodlot
